definition: Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use.
ruleOfThumb: If implementers keep adding 'not supported' methods, split the interface.

citation.author: Robert C. Martin
citation.bookTitle: Agile Software Development: Principles, Patterns, and Practices

questions.prompt: What's the clearest signal this interface is poorly sliced?

options.text: BuildBot must implement operations it cannot meaningfully support, so it depends on interface members unrelated to its role
options.explanation: The interface mixes human-team behaviors and automation behaviors. That forces some implementers to fake or reject methods, indicating the interface is too broad.

options.text: Interfaces should never contain more than three methods
options.explanation: Method count is not the principle. An interface can have many methods if every client needs them.

options.text: Throwing errors in method bodies is always incorrect in object-oriented design
options.explanation: Throws can be valid in many contexts. Here they are a symptom that the abstraction is forcing unrelated behavior.

options.text: The interface should be a type alias instead, since classes overcomplicate this design
options.explanation: Alias vs interface syntax is not the core issue. The problem is coupling unrelated capabilities into one contract.

questions.prompt: A mobile app only needs read access, but it must import this whole API contract. What design risk does that create?

options.text: The read-only client is coupled to write/delete/reset operations it never uses, so changes to admin behavior can ripple into mobile code unnecessarily
options.explanation: Even if MobileDirectory only calls listUsers, it still depends on a broad contract. Splitting read and admin interfaces reduces needless coupling and rebuild risk.

options.text: The issue is that search() should cache listUsers() to avoid repeated network calls
options.explanation: Caching can help performance, but it does not address the abstraction boundary problem described.

options.text: Promises are the problem; synchronous interfaces avoid this kind of coupling
options.explanation: Sync vs async does not change whether clients are forced to depend on methods they do not need.

options.text: The contract is acceptable because TypeScript tree-shaking removes unused interface members
options.explanation: Tree-shaking affects runtime bundles, not design-level coupling and dependency churn.

questions.prompt: Which design better protects small clients from unrelated API changes?

options.text: Version A — every client must depend on read, write, publish, and archive together, even if it needs only one capability
options.explanation: Broad interfaces create unnecessary compile-time and conceptual coupling. Version B lets each client depend only on the contract it actually uses.

options.text: Version B — splitting interfaces increases the chance of inconsistent behavior and should be avoided
options.explanation: Consistency should be enforced through tests and composition, not by forcing all capabilities into one interface.
